title: SBAR Hand-off and Safe Transfer of Care: Shift-Change Report on a Stable Post-Op Patient
subtitle: Hand-off (shift report / transfer of care) is a high-risk moment when communication gaps cause errors. SBAR — Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation — gives a clear, complete, structured report. This case follows a routine shift-change hand-off of a stable post-op day 1 patient: organizing the report into SBAR, including the essentials (allergies, code status, IV access, pending results), using read-back, and avoiding subjective labels and interruptions.

patient.name: Ronald Mercer
patient.code: Full code; stable post-op day 1 — routine end-of-shift SBAR hand-off to the oncoming nurse
patient.admitDate: Post-op day 1 after an uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy. He is stable, comfortable (pain 3/10), tolerating sips, voiding, and ambulating with assistance. History of type 2 diabetes and hypertension. The off-going nurse is giving a shift-change hand-off using SBAR.
patient.room: Med-surg unit; saline-lock IV left forearm; sliding-scale insulin; pending evening blood culture result

vitals.temp: 37.2°C; reported incisional pain 6/10, medicated with effect
37.0°C; ambulated in hall with assist, tolerated well
36.9°C; incisions clean and dry, voiding without difficulty
37.0°C; stable and comfortable at end of shift
37.0°C; both nurses verify the patient, IV site, and orders together

labs.v: 11.2 g/dL — mildly low post-op but stable, no signs of bleeding.
labs.r: Report as part of Assessment; flag the trend, not just the number.
Normal; no infection concern at hand-off.
Mention in Recommendation as a possible recheck.

labs.v: 142 mg/dL — mildly elevated, on sliding-scale insulin.
labs.r: Report current value and the insulin plan.

labs.v: Situation (who/why now), Background (relevant history/context), Assessment (current findings/concerns), Recommendation (what to do/follow up).
labs.r: SBAR keeps hand-off clear, complete, and prioritized.

labs.v: Always include identity/diagnosis, code status, allergies, IV access, current status, meds/pending results, and active concerns.
labs.r: Omitting code status, allergies, or pending results is a classic hand-off error.

labs.v: Use read-back of key items, clarify questions, and ideally hand off at the bedside verifying the patient and lines.
labs.r: Read-back and bedside verification catch errors before they reach the patient.

labs.v: Avoid subjective labels ('difficult'), gossip, and giving report where it can be overheard (privacy).
labs.r: Report objective, relevant, confidential information only.

labs.v: Evening blood culture result still pending at hand-off.
labs.r: Pending results MUST be communicated so follow-up is not lost.

nursesNote: Ronald Mercer, 68, post-op day 1 after uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy; stable, pain 3/10, ambulating with assist, voiding, incisions clean/dry. PMH type 2 diabetes, HTN. Allergy penicillin (rash). Full code. Saline-lock IV left forearm; sliding-scale insulin. Labs: Hgb 11.2 (low-stable), K 3.6, glucose 142; evening blood culture PENDING. End-of-shift hand-off using SBAR — include identity/diagnosis, code status, allergies, IV access, status, meds, pending culture, and concerns; use read-back and bedside verification; keep it objective and confidential.
providerOrders: Routine post-op day 1 care; advance diet and activity as tolerated
Sliding-scale insulin per protocol; recheck morning glucose
Continue saline-lock; normal saline if needed
Pain management; monitor incisions for bleeding/infection
Follow up pending evening blood culture result

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for a safe hand-off?
steps.inst: Select ALL items that should be communicated or considered.

opts.text: Identity, diagnosis, and that he is post-op day 1 and stable
opts.rat: Core Situation information for the oncoming nurse.

opts.text: Code status (full code) and allergy to penicillin
opts.rat: Safety essentials that must never be omitted.

opts.text: IV access, current meds (sliding-scale insulin), and the pending blood culture
opts.rat: Access, meds, and pending results must be handed off so follow-up is not lost.

opts.text: Current status — stable vitals, pain 3/10, ambulating, incisions clean/dry
opts.rat: The Assessment the oncoming nurse needs as a baseline.

opts.text: Recommendations — recheck glucose and possibly potassium, monitor incision, follow up culture
opts.rat: Clear next steps complete the SBAR report.

opts.text: A subjective label that he is a 'difficult, needy' patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ Subjective labels bias care and are inappropriate in hand-off.

opts.text: Skip report and let the oncoming nurse read the chart to save time
opts.rat: ⚠️ A verbal structured hand-off is the standard; chart-only is unsafe.

opts.text: SBAR organizes report into Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation
opts.rat: Correct — the structure that keeps hand-off complete.

opts.text: Code status, allergies, and pending results are essential hand-off elements
opts.rat: Correct — omitting them is a classic, dangerous error.

opts.text: Read-back and bedside verification reduce hand-off errors
opts.rat: Correct — confirming key items catches mistakes early.

opts.text: Report should be objective, relevant, and confidential
opts.rat: Correct — avoid labels, gossip, and being overheard.

opts.text: Pending results can be left out since the next shift will see them eventually
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — unspoken pending results are a leading cause of lost follow-up.

opts.text: A complete hand-off is unnecessary for a stable patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — stable patients still require a structured hand-off.

opts.text: Identify the patient, diagnosis, and current situation
opts.rat: #1 — Situation orients the oncoming nurse first.

opts.text: Communicate safety essentials — code status and allergies
opts.rat: #2 — Critical safety data given early.

opts.text: Give the current assessment and stability
opts.rat: #3 — Baseline status for the next shift.

opts.text: Hand off meds, IV access, and pending results
opts.rat: #4 — Ongoing care and follow-up items.

opts.text: State recommendations and confirm with read-back
opts.rat: #5 — Close the loop with next steps and verification.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Sort each statement into its SBAR component
steps.inst: Classify each item as Situation, Background, Assessment, or Recommendation.

opts.text: 'This is Ronald Mercer, 68, post-op day 1 after a lap cholecystectomy; I'm handing off his care.'
opts.rat: Who the patient is and why we are reporting now.

opts.text: 'Right now he is stable and comfortable, pain 3/10.'
opts.rat: The immediate situation/status snapshot.

opts.text: 'He has type 2 diabetes and hypertension; surgery was uncomplicated.'
opts.rat: Relevant history/context.

opts.text: 'He is allergic to penicillin and is a full code.'
opts.rat: Essential background safety data.

opts.text: 'He has a saline-lock IV in the left forearm with a normal-saline order if needed.'
opts.rat: Access and standing orders are background.

opts.text: 'Vitals are stable; morning glucose was 142 and potassium 3.6.'
opts.rat: Current findings/data.

opts.text: 'Lungs clear, incisions clean and dry, voiding, ambulating with assist.'
opts.rat: Current objective assessment.

opts.text: 'Hgb is 11.2, slightly low post-op but stable with no signs of bleeding.'
opts.rat: Current finding with interpretation.

opts.text: 'Please continue the sliding-scale insulin and recheck a morning glucose; potassium may need rechecking.'
opts.rat: What to do next.

opts.text: 'Monitor the incision and pain, encourage ambulation, and follow up the pending blood culture this evening.'
opts.rat: Follow-up actions to complete the hand-off.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: State the Situation — patient, diagnosis, current status
opts.rat: #1 — Orient the oncoming nurse.

opts.text: Give the Background — history, allergies, code status, access
opts.rat: #2 — Provide essential context and safety data.

opts.text: Report the Assessment — vitals, labs, current findings
opts.rat: #3 — Share the baseline and any concerns.

opts.text: Make Recommendations — meds, pending results, follow-up
opts.rat: #4 — Define next steps.

opts.text: Verify at the bedside and use read-back to confirm
opts.rat: #5 — Close the loop and catch errors.

opts.text: The nurse used SBAR and included code status, allergies, and the pending culture
opts.rat: Complete, structured, safe hand-off.

opts.text: The nurses verified the patient, IV site, and orders together at the bedside
opts.rat: Bedside verification reduces errors.

opts.text: The oncoming nurse read back the insulin plan and pending follow-up
opts.rat: Read-back confirms shared understanding.

opts.text: Report was given objectively without labels and in a private area
opts.rat: Objective and confidential — correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse skipped code status and allergies because the patient is stable
opts.rat: ⚠️ These are always essential, regardless of stability.

opts.text: The pending blood culture was not mentioned
opts.rat: ⚠️ Unspoken pending results lead to lost follow-up.

opts.text: The nurse described the patient as 'difficult and needy' instead of objective findings
opts.rat: ⚠️ Subjective labels bias care and are unprofessional.

opts.text: Report was given loudly in the hallway where visitors could hear
opts.rat: ⚠️ Violates patient privacy/confidentiality.
